title: Entick v Carrington
text:
Title: Entick v Carrington
Citation: (1765) 19 St Tr 1029
Jurisdiction: uk
Source URL: https://www.bailii.org/

The court held that executive officials needed legal authority for searches and seizures and could not rely on general state necessity. Entick is a foundational UK authority for legality, limits on executive power, property, privacy, and the rule of law.

title: Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corporation
source_name: Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corporation
text:
Title: Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corporation
Citation: [1948] 1 KB 223
Jurisdiction: uk
Source URL: https://www.bailii.org/

The Court of Appeal formulated the classic standard of irrationality in judicial review. A public authority decision may be unlawful if it is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could have made it, subject to later development through proportionality and rights-based review.

title: Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission
source_name: Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission
text:
Title: Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission
Citation: [1969] 2 AC 147
Jurisdiction: uk
Source URL: https://www.bailii.org/

The House of Lords treated errors of law by a public body as jurisdictional and resisted broad ouster of judicial review. Anisminic is central to rule-of-law review, statutory interpretation of ouster clauses, and the modern decline of the jurisdictional/non-jurisdictional error distinction.

title: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department, ex parte Fire Brigades Union
source_name: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department, ex parte Fire Brigades Union
text:
Title: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department, ex parte Fire Brigades Union
Citation: [1995] 2 AC 513
Jurisdiction: uk
Source URL: https://www.bailii.org/

The House of Lords held that a minister could not use prerogative power to frustrate a statutory scheme that Parliament had enacted but not yet brought into force. The case is important for prerogative powers, legitimate expectation, and parliamentary sovereignty.

title: R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union
source_name: R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union
text:
Title: R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union
Citation: [2017] UKSC 5
Jurisdiction: uk
Source URL: https://www.supremecourt.uk/cases/uksc-2016-0196.html

The UK Supreme Court held that ministers could not trigger Article 50 using prerogative powers without parliamentary authorization because withdrawal would alter domestic law and statutory rights. The case is central to prerogative power, parliamentary sovereignty, and Brexit constitutional law.

title: R (Miller) v Prime Minister; Cherry v Advocate General
source_name: R (Miller) v Prime Minister; Cherry v Advocate General
text:
Title: R (Miller) v Prime Minister; Cherry v Advocate General
Citation: [2019] UKSC 41
Jurisdiction: uk
Source URL: https://www.supremecourt.uk/cases/uksc-2019-0192.html

The Supreme Court held that prorogation was justiciable and unlawful because it frustrated Parliament's constitutional functions without reasonable justification. The case is a leading authority on constitutional principles, accountability, and limits of prerogative power.
